speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is take two. The date is January 16, 1997. I am conducting an interview with Barbara Marlow. The location is London, England. Barbara, you were telling us about Germans coming into the home and removing things. How did that seem to you as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange. Strange. But as a child, you accept things. One occasion, though, stands out in my mind. On that occasion, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans came in, and they decided to remove a dining room suite which had belonged to my uncle. His flat had been bombed, and some of his things were brought to my grandparents' flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: On this occasion, they started-- the soldiers started carrying the furniture away, when I suddenly realized that in the sideboard drawer, I had left my stamp collection. Now, that upset me very much, because it was something which belonged to me, and I wanted to have it. So I rushed to my grandmother and said, they've got my stamps, can you get them? And she spoke very good German. She had been educated. She went to boarding school in Germany in her younger days. And she went out onto the stairs and said to the soldier, my little granddaughter had left her stamps-- could she have them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he turned around and hit her across her face. And that is something which really frightened me and shocked me. Proof, that I remember it more than 60 years on. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The other thing was that there were big notices on the entrance to the apartment, saying that this was a Jewish apartment, and that nothing should be taken away without permission, because all the contents belonged to the German Reich, or whatever it-- but we were not supposed to take anything other than personal belongings out of the apartment. And other Germans could come and take things away, but nothing belonged to us anymore. But other than that, of course, I couldn't go to school. And I don't think I had any lessons at that time, so that was strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I really can't remember what happened that-- that year. And then, of course, later that year--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940. I-- probably-- it was September. We had to move, because the Germans formed a ghetto in Warsaw. Now, one thing which I had not mentioned before. At the beginning of 1940, our family converted-- my parents, my brother, and myself. I never asked them why it happened at that time, but I have recently found a document which explains it, partly. It was a piece of paper from the priest at the church where we converted, certifying that my father's a Roman Catholic. And I think that, taking the timing into account, it must have been a document which was required for him to obtain his passport. But I never asked him about it afterwords. Why it happened. It seemed quite a natural thing to have happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the process that you went through in order to convert?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. I-- I don't think so. I mean, I knew all the prayers from school, in any case. So I don't know what was the process, as far as my parents were concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any other Jewish families doing the same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It wasn't something which one discussed. A lot of my friends had left in '39, you know, when people were leaving Warsaw, and I never saw them again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. I really can't answer that question. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you talked about the formation of the ghetto in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had to move. And that was sort of the first shock, because from a very large apartment, where I had my own room and lots of space, we suddenly had to share a flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. We just had two rooms, one for my mother and myself, and one for my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there, from your flat to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I mean, I probably-- if I remember, I went for a walk, and I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what you had with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only personal things. I mean, no furniture. Just personal belongings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take your doll?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I can't really remember very much about it. I know that it wasn't very comfortable. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an elderly-- there was an elderly couple, an engineer and his wife-- I don't remember their name-- who lived there. They were childless, so they didn't take very kindly to having a young child in the flat. My grandmother, who previously had a cook, took over the housekeeping, but she had to share the kitchen. There were arguments. It was not very pleasant. At that time, the flat was in a house which-- the front of which was in the ghetto, and the sort of second courtyard was on the Aryan side. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] An old friend of my mother's, whose daughter was at nursery school with my brother, made contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Mrs. Stanislawa Wedecka. Made contact with my mother in 1940 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and offered any help she could give her. And she-- this lady used to come and throw food over the wall from the window in the second sort of courtyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did she throw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. All I know is that she used to bring us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this at night, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I think it was in daytime. But the-- well, the Germans couldn't keep track of everything. And obviously, nobody objected in the other house to her doing it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, there was much less variety of food in the ghetto. I was-- I was never hungry, but I do remember an occasion where I was given sweets, which I absolutely hated. And I got told on, because I said to my grandmother, this is food fit-- fit for pigs, not for humans, [CHUCKLES] which was not very appropriate at that time. But I was a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: So things did change for me, of course. But once we were in the ghetto, I joined a group of other children for lessons and other various activities. I went to art classes. I was having English lessons. I was kept occupied as much as possible and out of everybody's way. And what was your mother doing during-- while you were at school or learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. Probably what she always did, met her friend. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't-- I really can't answer--

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like a reasonably normal life at the very beginning, the way you're describing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a reasonably normal life, except that the environment was abnormal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the environment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, we were in one room, with my mother, which I had never done before. There was no space. There was no park. I couldn't go out and play. There were no green spaces in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So from that point of view, things were-- were different. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it wasn't very long before the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took out parts of the ghetto. Reduced the ghetto in size. And that was quite traumatic for us, because there was even less accommodation. And it was impossible for us to find anywhere, two rooms in one place. So that was a sort of first separation from my grandparents. My mother and I got one room in one house. My grandparents were somewhere else. We used to see them every day. My grandmother was still housekeeping for the family, so we always had lunch and dinner with her. But that was the first change. And of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- the smaller the ghetto got, the more crowded it became. And the atmosphere was changing. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They were already bringing in, I think, Jews from smaller surrounding areas. So the people around me were becoming more and more alien, in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were more sort of Hasidic Jews, people who would dress differently, had much more Yiddish, which was a foreign language to me. I couldn't understand what they were talking about. They looked different. So for a child, it was a strange environment. Also, we were now-- the place where we were living faced a wall. And that meant that at night, which was a time when food was being smuggled into the ghetto, there were small children bringing in bread at night, making holes in the wall and bringing in sacks of potatoes, or whatever. And the Germans were shooting at night, so the nights were very disturbed. You could see, suddenly, flood lights and shooting. On one occasion, the Germans came to the house, and in one of the apartments lived a journalist. I think his name was Warszawski. And he was an old friend of my uncle, who was also a journalist. And they took him and his father out and shot them in the doorway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it, but I heard it in the morning. I mean, I heard the Germans coming in. And then I knew that in the morning, there was talk that they had been shot. So there was-- every time somebody started banging on the gate, everybody was scared, because we just didn't know what was going to happen. So there was this constant uncertainty. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was the winter-- either of 1940 or '41 was a very, very severe winter. And the Germans used to switch off electricity at night. I think it was 7 or 8 o'clock. So we had to use carbide lamps, which were horrible. And, of course, there was a great shortage of fuel in the ghetto, so it was very cold, and I had horrible frostbite on my hands and feet. I mean, they were sort of open frostbite, which were very unpleasant and painful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The amount of food decreased. I still wasn't hungry, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things were you eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was bread, which wasn't very nice. And everything was what we called ersatz-- tea, coffee. There were no sweets, no chocolate. Jam was made out of carrots or beet roots. All these sort of things. There was-- I think there was very little meat that I remember. And fresh vegetables were limited. You wouldn't have any salad stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the Jewish council organizations that were arranging soup kitchens, and that sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But I became very aware of people being very hungry and people dying in the streets of hunger, because you could see them. And in 1941, in the summer, I left the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish locals were half in the ghetto and half on the Aryan side, so you could go in and mingle. I'm sure that they were watching you, but that summer, I went there with my mother. And Mrs. Biedrzycka came and took me away. And I had a month in the country. It wasn't an unqualified success, because I developed measles. [LAUGHS] So I was pretty miserable, being on my own. I can't-- it must have been a sort of children's colony, but nobody knew I was Jewish, of course. But I was on my own, and I was ill, so I was rather unhappy. And another event which happened we came-- when I came back is, I had brought a little basket of strawberries for my grandmother and my mother. And we were walking back from the locals--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw 'we,' you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and I. And I was carrying this little basket, when, suddenly, it was snatched from me. And this was something which was happening a lot, but it was the only time it happened to me. People who were very hungry and starving-- if they saw somebody walking with what looked like food, they would jump them. And to make sure that the food wouldn't be taken away from them, they would literally go at it like a dog with their mouth, bite into it, and run away. And that's what's happened to me. My basket of strawberries was snatched in this way, and that was a great shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I was upset. I cried, but what could one do? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, it was explained to me that the people-- some people were very hungry, and some--

speaker: interviewee
text: And desperate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And desperate. And I had seen bodies in the street covered with newspaper, not covered. Not covered with newspaper, and they were like skeletons. And every morning, carts came around and collected them off the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I mean, this was a sort of reality. I didn't know what I felt about it. I think I just accepted it, the same as I accepted the changed environment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there were the walls all around the ghetto, and there were gates through which designated people came. The ghetto was divided into two parts. There was a little ghetto and the big ghetto. We were living in the area which as a little ghetto, which was sort of closer to the Aryan side. The big ghetto was the Old Jewish Quarter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A tram went through the middle, between the two-- street which separated the two. And to go from one to the other, you went over a bridge, which went over that street. It was built by the Germans. And at that gate, there was a German soldier who, when he was on duty-- so everybody said, you know, be careful. Don't go there. Because he used to just take potshots at-- for no reason. I think they called him 'Mad Hans,' but I'm not sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But all these things just happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to your mother about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did she talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember that. No. I think, you know, as I said, I was pretty occupied. There was one small garden, which was attached to a church in [NON-ENGLISH]. And in the almshouses around it lived various families who we knew. So I used to go there and play. That was a sort of-- one bit of fresh air which you could enjoy. But other than that, I had my lessons, or I was at home with my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were they coping by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, I think. All right. That went until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December 1941, when my grandfather, who, although he was-- had been retired for several years from the hospital where he used to work, was still doing some work in the ghetto. And he went to see a patient, came back, and said the place was filthy. He was quite upset. And a couple of days later, or a few days later, he developed typhoid [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he died within a week. And that was a great shock to me, because I was particularly attached to my grandfather. He was a wonderful person-- the sort of person-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you just looked at him, and you felt better. He was a great physician, and the kindest person you could imagine. And I remember going to see him for the last time. We had just had a letter from my father, and we took it. I don't know how it arrived. It probably arrived by Mrs. Biedrzycka and probably Switzerland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother and I went to see him. I wasn't allowed into the room. The door just opened, and Grandmother said, Ludwig, [? Bashak ?] has come to see you. And he just lifted his hand, and that was the last time I saw him. I was allowed to go to his funeral, which was one of the last funerals which were allowed to take place with all the participants going to the cemetery. Because the Jewish-- In the cemetery outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish cemetery was outside the ghetto, but we were allowed to go there. And I remember wonderful speeches by his colleagues and the funeral. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, one of my mother's sisters, who was divorced and on her own, moved in with my grandmother to look after her. And we still lived in the previous apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that brings us to the summer of 1942 and the beginning of the liquidation of the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that time, I was already coming up to 12, so I was much more aware of what was going on. And I-- there were notices all over the place, inviting people to report voluntarily for deportation to the east and being promised bread and jam if they went voluntarily. And some people went, especially desperate ones, the ones who were hungry. There were great discussions going on. There was a wonderful teacher I had in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it was Miss Dorothy. But I think it was Marienstadt Morganstadt, something of that kind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- it was a group that was [NON-ENGLISH], Boden, whose father was killed by the Russians in Katyn Forest. And there was Elisa Regirer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember. There was Elizabeth, who was a niece of our teacher. I think there were four other-- I can't remember any other names. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she and her niece and whoever was in the family, I don't remember, they decided to report. People were saying, if we go voluntarily, then it will be better than if we are rounded up. But the number of volunteers dwindled, and the Germans resorted to rounding people up for deportation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, it was said that if you were working for the German war effort, you would not be deported. So I don't know who set up workshops all over the place, including one behind that church, which I mentioned before-- and people were given [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certificates that they were working for the German war effort. My mother had one, my grandmother, and my aunt. There were all supposed to be attached to the one at this church hall. But of course, I was too young to have one. And I know there was an occasion when my mother went to see the president of the Jewish Council, Adam Chernikov, to ask if he could do anything to help my grandmother, because she was getting [INAUDIBLE] that she was elderly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she came back very saddened. She said, I was in that room, where over his desk was a portrait of great-grandfather. And he said to her, I get hundreds of people everyday coming with the same request-- there is nothing I can do. At that time, we felt very bitter about it. From my reading of history, there really wasn't very much he could have done.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to-- [SIGHS] I think, must have gone on for two or three weeks, the deportations, when on one occasion, a friend who was living, again, in the almshouses, told my mother that the following day, the Germans were preparing an action in that area. And he suggested that my mother and I spend the night with him and his wife. And in the morning, he would take us to the police headquarters, where the families of the policemen were sheltering and supposed to be safe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That morning, we left about 5-- we got ready about 5 o'clock in the morning. My grandmother and aunt were already in the workshop, and my mother said-- told me to go and say goodbye to grandmother. I remember going in, and grandmother came out. We were behind the church when I said, Mommy said to say goodbye. And she handed me a gold bracelet, which afterwards, my mother said had been bought for security as capital. She gave it to me and said, take this. And I said, Mommy didn't tell me to take anything. And I handed it back to her and ran off. We had a rickshaw, which was a means of transportation in the ghetto. And we were in a rickshaw going to the big ghetto, to the police headquarters, when German units passed us on the way to the action. We spent a whole day there, and then in the evening-- the Germans were very organized. They only did the action between certain hours. So the nights were quiet. They were sleeping, and so were we. So once the action had finished, we went back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that is another of my very, very, vivid memories, when we arrived at the workshop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stop there?
